content_of_post_to_comment:
I make products. Over the last few years I've built and shipped a handful of them. On paper I did the work — but honestly, not a single one came close to what I hoped for.

For a long time I blamed the ideas, the market, the timing. But looking back, the real problem was focus. I could never stay locked on one thing for long. I'd start on fire, fizzle out after a few weeks, jump to the next shiny idea, and quietly abandon the last one. Over and over.

Pomodoro wasn't useless for me — breaking work into 25-minute chunks did help. But the thing I felt most clearly was this: I focus way better when I'm doing it alongside someone else. More energy than working alone, and I'm far less likely to bail in the middle.

And here's the part that actually stings: my problem was never making a plan — it's that I don't follow the plan I make. I plan well and execute badly.

Two things I'd genuinely love to hear:

Have you ever focused better just because you were doing something alongside someone else? (study sessions, coworking, working over a call — anything)

For those who plan well but don't follow through — what actually helped you close that gap?
comment: I've noticed the same thing with side projects: accountability often beats motivation. A small change that helped me was making the plan public to one person and having a fixed check-in, because vague intentions are easy to abandon. Also, sometimes the issue isn't discipline but keeping too many projects emotionally 'alive' at once.
